metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Workplace Mathematics 10 connects mathematical reasoning to practical, real-world contexts relevant to workplace and everyday life. Students apply proportional reasoning, trigonometry, measurement, data analysis, and financial literacy to solve problems encountered in trades, service industries, and daily decision-making. This course opens pathways into Workplace Mathematics 11 and 12, and supports students in applied and trades programs.
metadata.general_description: A practical mathematics course applying proportional reasoning, trigonometry, measurement, data analysis, and financial literacy to workplace and everyday contexts — supporting applied and trades-related post-secondary pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Proportional reasoning is employed to understand multiplicative relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reasoning about relative size and scale — rather than absolute numerical difference — allows us to make sense of ratios, rates, and multiplicative comparisons in practical contexts.

big_ideas.statement: 3D objects can be examined mathematically by measuring directly and indirectly length, surface area, and volume.
big_ideas.elaboration: Three-dimensional objects encountered in the workplace and built environment can be described, measured, and analyzed using geometric formulas and spatial reasoning.

big_ideas.statement: Developing adaptability with numbers builds meaning, understanding, and confidence.
big_ideas.elaboration: Numerical fluency — including estimation, mental math, and flexible computation — builds the confidence and competence to tackle real-world mathematical challenges.

big_ideas.statement: Representing and analyzing data allows us to notice and wonder about relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Creating, reading, and critically evaluating graphs and data displays allows us to draw meaningful conclusions and identify trends relevant to workplace and social decision-making.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, and strategic thinking about number; model mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively and with curiosity when exploring problems.
i_can_statements: I can develop thinking strategies to approach and solve practical mathematical problems.
I can explore and apply mathematical ideas using reasoning, technology, and appropriate tools.
I can estimate reasonably in workplace and everyday measurement contexts.
I can model real-world situations mathematically and use my model to solve problems.
I can approach unfamiliar problems with curiosity and creative thinking.

competencies.raw: Develop and apply conceptual understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize mathematical concepts and relationships; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve problems with persistence and positive disposition; engage in problem-solving connected with place, story, and cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can apply mathematical understanding to solve practical, workplace, and everyday problems.
I can visualize three-dimensional objects and spatial relationships to support problem solving.
I can apply flexible strategies to solve problems, including drawing diagrams and using technology.
I can persist through challenging problems and maintain a positive attitude toward mathematics.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in classroom discussions; take risks when offering ideas in classroom discourse.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify my mathematical reasoning in practical contexts.
I can represent mathematical ideas using diagrams, tables, graphs, and symbols.
I can use appropriate mathematical vocabulary in discussions about workplace and everyday problems.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my mathematical approaches and what I might do differently.
I can connect mathematics to trades, workplace contexts, and my own interests.
I can view mistakes as valuable learning opportunities.
I can incorporate First Peoples knowledge and practices — including traditional measurement and spatial reasoning — into my mathematical thinking.

areas.name: Data and Graph Interpretation
topics: Types of graphs: bar, line, circle, histogram, scatter plot
Creating graphs from data: labelling, scale, and accuracy
Interpreting graphs: trends, predictions, and anomalies
Critiquing graphs: misleading scales, missing labels, biased representations
First Peoples data representations and storytelling through visual data

areas.name: Trigonometry
topics: Right triangle trigonometry: SOH-CAH-TOA
Finding missing sides using sine, cosine, and tangent ratios
Finding missing angles using inverse trigonometric functions
Applications: angles of elevation and depression, ramp slopes, roof pitch

areas.name: Measurement
areas.raw: Metric and imperial measurement and conversions; surface area and volume.
topics: Metric units: length, area, volume, mass — conversions within metric system
Imperial units: inches, feet, yards, miles, pounds, gallons — conversions
Metric-imperial conversions: practical applications
Surface area of prisms and cylinders
Volume of prisms and cylinders
Applications: construction, trades, packaging, and materials estimation

areas.name: Statistics and Probability
topics: Measures of central tendency: mean, median, mode, range
Weighted averages and their applications
Experimental probability: conducting trials, comparing to theoretical probability
Using probability in everyday contexts: weather, risk, insurance

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Financial literacy: income, taxes, deductions, gross and net pay.
topics: Employment income: wages, salary, piecework, commission, and overtime
Gross pay vs. net pay: understanding deductions
Payroll deductions: income tax, CPP, EI
Budgeting with take-home pay
Unit pricing and best buy comparisons in workplace contexts
